question_text: The genetic code is a triplet code, meaning each codon consists of:
explanation: The genetic code is a triplet code where each codon consists of three nucleotides (bases) on mRNA. George Gamow suggested that in order to code for 20 amino acids, the code must be made up of three nucleotides. With four bases (A, U, G, C), three-letter combinations give 4³ = 64 possible codons, which is more than sufficient to code for 20 amino acids.
subject: biology

elimination_hints.hint: A doublet code (two nucleotides per codon) would give only 4² = 16 combinations, which is insufficient to code for 20 amino acids.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing doublet and triplet codes

elimination_hints.hint: Four nucleotides per codon would give 4⁴ = 256 combinations — far more than needed and not the established genetic code system.
elimination_hints.misconception: Overestimating the number of bases per codon

elimination_hints.hint: Six nucleotides per codon would produce an unnecessarily large number of combinations (4⁶ = 4096). The genetic code uses a triplet system.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing codon size with larger nucleotide sequences

question_text: Which of the following is the start codon that also codes for methionine?
explanation: AUG is the universal start codon (initiation codon) that codes for the amino acid methionine. In prokaryotes, AUG codes for N-formylmethionine (fMet) during initiation, while in eukaryotes it codes for methionine. AUG serves the dual function of being the start signal for translation and coding for methionine wherever it appears in the mRNA sequence.
subject: biology

elimination_hints.hint: UAA is a stop codon (also called 'ochre'), not a start codon. It signals termination of translation.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing stop codons with start codons

elimination_hints.hint: UAG is a stop codon (also called 'amber'), not a start codon. It terminates translation.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing stop codons with start codons

elimination_hints.hint: UGA is a stop codon (also called 'opal'), not a start codon. It signals the end of translation.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing stop codons with start codons

question_text: The property of the genetic code where more than one codon can code for the same amino acid is called:
explanation: Degeneracy (also called redundancy) is the property of the genetic code where multiple codons can specify the same amino acid. Since there are 64 codons but only 20 amino acids (plus stop signals), some amino acids are coded by more than one codon. For example, leucine is coded by six different codons (UUA, UUG, CUU, CUC, CUA, CUG). This property is different from ambiguity — the genetic code is unambiguous because one codon always codes for only one amino acid.
subject: biology

elimination_hints.hint: Universality means the genetic code is the same in almost all organisms, from bacteria to humans. It does not describe multiple codons for one amino acid.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing universality with degeneracy of genetic code

elimination_hints.hint: The genetic code is unambiguous, meaning one codon codes for only one amino acid. Ambiguity would mean one codon could code for different amino acids, which does not occur.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing degeneracy (many-to-one) with ambiguity (one-to-many)

elimination_hints.hint: The genetic code is non-overlapping, meaning each nucleotide belongs to only one codon. This is a separate property from degeneracy.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing non-overlapping nature with degeneracy

question_text:
Consider the following statements about aminoacyl-tRNA synthetase:
(i) It recognizes both the amino acid and its cognate tRNA
(ii) It has a proofreading mechanism to ensure correct amino acid-tRNA pairing
(iii) There are exactly 61 different aminoacyl-tRNA synthetases, one for each sense codon
(iv) The reaction requires ATP and produces AMP + PPi

Which of the above statements are correct?
explanation: Statements (i), (ii), and (iv) are correct. Aminoacyl-tRNA synthetase recognizes both the specific amino acid and its cognate tRNA — this dual specificity is sometimes called the 'second genetic code' because the fidelity of translation depends on correct aminoacylation. The enzyme has a proofreading (editing) mechanism that hydrolyzes incorrectly attached amino acids. The charging reaction uses ATP, which is cleaved to AMP and pyrophosphate (PPi), making it energetically equivalent to using two high-energy bonds. Statement (iii) is incorrect because there are only about 20 different aminoacyl-tRNA synthetases (one for each amino acid), not 61. A single synthetase can charge all the isoaccepting tRNAs (multiple tRNAs) for the same amino acid.
subject: biology

elimination_hints.hint: This includes statement (iii), which is incorrect. There are approximately 20 aminoacyl-tRNA synthetases (one per amino acid), not 61.
elimination_hints.misconception: Assuming one synthetase per codon rather than one per amino acid

elimination_hints.hint: This includes statement (iii), which is incorrect. There are about 20 synthetases, not 61. A single synthetase charges all isoaccepting tRNAs for the same amino acid.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the number of sense codons with the number of synthetases

elimination_hints.hint: This excludes statement (i) and includes (iii). Statement (i) is correct — the enzyme must recognize both the amino acid and its cognate tRNA. Statement (iii) is incorrect.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not recognizing the dual specificity of aminoacyl-tRNA synthetase

question_text: Which of the following stop codons is also known as 'amber'?
explanation: UAG is known as the 'amber' stop codon. The three stop codons have specific names: UAG is amber, UAA is ochre, and UGA is opal (or umber). These names originated from the history of their discovery in bacteriophage genetics. Stop codons do not code for any amino acid and instead signal the termination of polypeptide synthesis during translation.
subject: biology

elimination_hints.hint: UAA is a stop codon but it is called 'ochre', not 'amber'.
elimination_hints.misconception: Mixing up the names of different stop codons

elimination_hints.hint: UGA is a stop codon but it is called 'opal' (or umber), not 'amber'.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the names associated with different stop codons

elimination_hints.hint: AUG is the start codon (initiation codon), not a stop codon. It codes for methionine.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing start codons with stop codons

question_text: The amino acid attached to the 3' end of tRNA is linked at which specific sequence?
explanation: The amino acid is attached to the 3' end of tRNA at the CCA sequence (also called the acceptor arm or acceptor stem). All tRNAs have the universal CCA trinucleotide sequence at their 3' end. The amino acid is covalently linked to the adenine (A) residue of CCA through an ester bond. This attachment is catalyzed by aminoacyl-tRNA synthetase in an ATP-dependent reaction called charging or aminoacylation of tRNA.
subject: biology

elimination_hints.hint: AAC is not the universal 3' terminal sequence of tRNA. The correct sequence is CCA.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the order of nucleotides at the 3' end of tRNA

elimination_hints.hint: ACC is not the correct sequence. The 3' terminal trinucleotide of all tRNAs is CCA, not ACC.
elimination_hints.misconception: Reversing or confusing the nucleotide sequence at the acceptor end

elimination_hints.hint: GCA is not the terminal sequence of tRNA. All functional tRNAs have the invariant CCA sequence at the 3' end.
elimination_hints.misconception: Incorrectly recalling the acceptor end sequence of tRNA

question_text: During translation, the formation of peptide bonds between amino acids is catalyzed by:
explanation: Peptide bond formation during translation is catalyzed by peptidyl transferase, which is a ribozyme (RNA enzyme) activity of the 23S rRNA in the larger subunit of the ribosome. This was a significant discovery as it showed that RNA, not protein, catalyzes this crucial step. The peptidyl transferase activity resides in the 50S subunit of prokaryotic ribosomes (23S rRNA) and in the 60S subunit of eukaryotic ribosomes (28S rRNA).
subject: biology

elimination_hints.hint: Aminoacyl-tRNA synthetase charges tRNA with the correct amino acid (aminoacylation), but it does not catalyze peptide bond formation on the ribosome.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing tRNA charging with peptide bond formation

elimination_hints.hint: DNA polymerase synthesizes DNA during replication. It plays no role in translation or peptide bond formation.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing enzymes of replication with those of translation

elimination_hints.hint: RNA polymerase catalyzes transcription (RNA synthesis from DNA template), not translation or peptide bond formation.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing transcription enzyme with translation enzyme

question_text: Har Gobind Khorana's contribution to deciphering the genetic code involved:
explanation: Har Gobind Khorana developed the method of chemically synthesizing specific RNA molecules (polynucleotides) with defined sequences and using them in cell-free translation systems to determine which codons code for which amino acids. He synthesized RNA molecules with repeating sequences of known composition, which helped assign specific codons to amino acids. Marshall Nirenberg, on the other hand, developed the cell-free translation system and used synthetic polynucleotides like poly-U to show that UUU codes for phenylalanine. Severo Ochoa synthesized polyribonucleotides using polynucleotide phosphorylase.
subject: biology

options.text: Developing the cell-free translation system using poly-U
Chemically synthesizing specific RNA molecules with defined sequences
Discovering the double helical structure of DNA

elimination_hints.hint: The cell-free translation system and the poly-U experiment were developed by Marshall Nirenberg and Heinrich Matthaei, not Khorana.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing Nirenberg's contribution with Khorana's contribution

elimination_hints.hint: The double helical structure of DNA was proposed by Watson and Crick in 1953, not by Khorana.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing DNA structure discovery with genetic code deciphering

elimination_hints.hint: The wobble hypothesis was proposed by Francis Crick in 1966, not by Khorana.
elimination_hints.misconception: Attributing Crick's wobble hypothesis to Khorana

question_text: In the wobble hypothesis proposed by Crick, non-standard base pairing occurs at which position of the codon?
explanation: The wobble hypothesis, proposed by Francis Crick in 1966, states that the base pairing between the third position of the codon (on mRNA, read in the 5'→3' direction) and the first position of the anticodon (on tRNA, read in the 3'→5' direction) is less strict than standard Watson-Crick base pairing. This 'wobble' at the third codon position allows a single tRNA to recognize more than one codon, which partly explains the degeneracy of the genetic code. For example, inosine (I) in the first position of the anticodon can pair with U, C, or A at the third position of the codon.
subject: biology

elimination_hints.hint: The first position of the codon follows strict Watson-Crick base pairing. Wobble occurs only at the third position.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the first position with the wobble position

elimination_hints.hint: The second position of the codon follows strict Watson-Crick base pairing and is the most critical for amino acid specificity. Wobble does not occur here.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the second codon position with the wobble position

elimination_hints.hint: Wobble base pairing is specific to the third position of the codon only. The first and second positions follow standard Watson-Crick pairing.
elimination_hints.misconception: Incorrectly generalizing wobble to all codon positions

question_text: During the initiation of translation in prokaryotes, the smaller ribosomal subunit first binds to mRNA at the:
explanation: In prokaryotes, the 30S (smaller) ribosomal subunit binds to the Shine-Dalgarno sequence on the mRNA during translation initiation. The Shine-Dalgarno sequence is a purine-rich region located upstream (5' side) of the start codon (AUG). It is complementary to the 3' end of the 16S rRNA in the 30S subunit. This base pairing helps position the ribosome correctly at the start codon. After the initiator tRNA (carrying fMet) binds to the AUG codon, the 50S subunit joins to form the complete 70S initiation complex.
subject: biology

elimination_hints.hint: The poly-A tail is found at the 3' end of eukaryotic mRNA and is involved in mRNA stability and export, not in prokaryotic ribosome binding.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing eukaryotic mRNA features with prokaryotic translation initiation

elimination_hints.hint: The 5' cap (7-methylguanosine) is a feature of eukaryotic mRNA. Prokaryotic mRNA lacks a 5' cap.
elimination_hints.misconception: Applying eukaryotic mRNA features to prokaryotic translation

elimination_hints.hint: The Kozak sequence is the ribosome binding site in eukaryotic mRNA, not in prokaryotic mRNA. Prokaryotes use the Shine-Dalgarno sequence.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing eukaryotic Kozak sequence with prokaryotic Shine-Dalgarno sequence

question_text: If a synthetic mRNA with repeating dinucleotide sequence 5'-UCUCUCUC...-3' is used in a cell-free translation system, how many types of amino acids will be incorporated into the polypeptides?
explanation: A repeating UC dinucleotide sequence (5'-UCUCUCUC...-3') can be read in two reading frames depending on where translation initiates: Frame 1 gives UCU-CUC-UCU-CUC... producing alternating serine (UCU) and leucine (CUC), and Frame 2 gives CUC-UCU-CUC-UCU... producing alternating leucine (CUC) and serine (UCU). In both reading frames, only two types of amino acids — serine and leucine — are incorporated. Therefore, two types of amino acids will be found in the polypeptides produced.
subject: biology

elimination_hints.hint: One amino acid would result from a repeating trinucleotide (like UUU-UUU giving only phenylalanine). A repeating dinucleotide produces two alternating codons, hence two amino acids.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing repeating dinucleotide with repeating single nucleotide patterns

elimination_hints.hint: Three amino acids would result from a repeating trinucleotide of three different bases (each frame gives a different codon). UC repeats give only two distinct codons across all frames.
elimination_hints.misconception: Incorrectly calculating the number of distinct codons from a repeating dinucleotide

elimination_hints.hint: Four amino acids cannot be generated from only two alternating nucleotides. Only two distinct codons (UCU and CUC) are possible from a UC repeat.
elimination_hints.misconception: Overestimating the number of codons from a simple repeating sequence

question_text: A mutation causes a change in the anticodon of a tRNA from 3'-UAC-5' to 3'-UAG-5'. This mutant tRNA will now insert its amino acid in response to which codon?
explanation: The anticodon on tRNA reads the codon on mRNA by antiparallel complementary base pairing. The original anticodon 3'-UAC-5' pairs with the codon 5'-AUG-3' (the start codon for methionine). When the anticodon mutates to 3'-UAG-5', it will now pair with the mRNA codon 5'-AUC-3' (by antiparallel complementary pairing: U pairs with A, A pairs with U, G pairs with C). AUC is the codon for isoleucine. So the mutant tRNA will incorrectly insert its amino acid (methionine) at positions where isoleucine should be placed, leading to a missense-type error at the translational level.
subject: biology

elimination_hints.hint: AUG was the original codon recognized by the unmutated anticodon 3'-UAC-5'. After mutation to 3'-UAG-5', it no longer pairs with AUG.
elimination_hints.misconception: Failing to account for the change in anticodon sequence

elimination_hints.hint: UAG is the mutant anticodon sequence itself, not the mRNA codon it pairs with. Anticodon-codon pairing is antiparallel and complementary.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the anticodon sequence with the codon it recognizes

elimination_hints.hint: CUA would require the anticodon 3'-GAU-5'. The mutant anticodon 3'-UAG-5' pairs with 5'-AUC-3', not CUA.
elimination_hints.misconception: Incorrectly reading the codon-anticodon pairing directionality

question_text:
Assertion (A): The genetic code is said to be degenerate.
Reason (R): Some amino acids are coded by more than one codon.

Select the correct option:
(A) Both A and R are correct and R is the correct explanation of A
(B) Both A and R are correct but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
(C) A is correct but R is incorrect
(D) A is incorrect but R is correct
explanation: The assertion is correct — the genetic code is indeed degenerate (also called redundant). The reason is also correct — some amino acids are coded by more than one codon (for example, leucine has 6 codons, serine has 6 codons, arginine has 6 codons). Furthermore, the reason directly explains the assertion: the genetic code is called degenerate precisely because multiple codons can specify the same amino acid. Out of 64 codons, 61 code for 20 amino acids, so many amino acids must be encoded by more than one codon. Therefore, R is the correct explanation of A.
subject: biology

options.text: Both A and R are correct and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are correct but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is correct but R is incorrect
A is incorrect but R is correct

elimination_hints.hint: The reason directly defines why the code is called degenerate. Multiple codons coding for the same amino acid IS the definition of degeneracy, so R is the correct explanation of A.
elimination_hints.misconception: Failing to recognize that the reason provides the direct definition of the assertion

elimination_hints.hint: The reason is factually correct. Many amino acids are indeed coded by more than one codon (e.g., leucine has 6 codons).
elimination_hints.misconception: Incorrectly thinking that each amino acid has only one codon

elimination_hints.hint: The assertion is correct. Degeneracy is a well-established property of the genetic code as described in NCERT and all standard biology textbooks.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not recognizing degeneracy as a valid property of the genetic code

question_text:
Assertion (A): In prokaryotes, translation can begin even before transcription of the mRNA is complete.
Reason (R): Prokaryotic mRNA does not require processing and the ribosome can attach to the 5' end of the nascent mRNA.

Select the correct option:
(A) Both A and R are correct and R is the correct explanation of A
(B) Both A and R are correct but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
(C) A is correct but R is incorrect
(D) A is incorrect but R is correct
explanation: The assertion is correct — in prokaryotes, translation and transcription are coupled, meaning ribosomes can begin translating the mRNA while it is still being transcribed by RNA polymerase. This is possible because prokaryotes lack a nuclear membrane, so transcription and translation occur in the same compartment (cytoplasm). The reason is also correct — prokaryotic mRNA does not undergo the extensive processing (5' capping, 3' polyadenylation, splicing) required for eukaryotic mRNA, and the ribosome can bind to the Shine-Dalgarno sequence at the 5' end of the nascent mRNA while the 3' end is still being synthesized. The reason directly explains why coupled transcription-translation is possible in prokaryotes, making R the correct explanation of A.
subject: biology

options.text: Both A and R are correct and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are correct but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is correct but R is incorrect
A is incorrect but R is correct

elimination_hints.hint: The reason directly explains how coupled transcription-translation is possible — because mRNA needs no processing, ribosomes can translate nascent mRNA. This is the mechanistic basis of the assertion.
elimination_hints.misconception: Failing to see the causal link between lack of mRNA processing and coupled transcription-translation

elimination_hints.hint: The reason is correct. Prokaryotic mRNA indeed lacks the extensive processing of eukaryotic mRNA (no 5' capping, no splicing in most cases), and ribosomes can attach to the 5' end of the growing mRNA.
elimination_hints.misconception: Incorrectly thinking that prokaryotic mRNA undergoes extensive processing like eukaryotic mRNA

elimination_hints.hint: The assertion is well-established. Coupled transcription-translation is a hallmark feature of prokaryotic gene expression, as described in NCERT.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not knowing that prokaryotic transcription and translation are coupled processes

question_text:
Match the following components of translation with their functions:

Column I:
(P) Aminoacyl-tRNA synthetase
(Q) Release factors
(R) Peptidyl transferase
(S) EF-Tu (in prokaryotes)

Column II:
(i) Catalyzes peptide bond formation
(ii) Charges tRNA with the correct amino acid
(iii) Delivers aminoacyl-tRNA to the ribosome A-site
(iv) Recognizes stop codons and terminates translation

Select the correct match:
explanation: The correct matching is: (P) Aminoacyl-tRNA synthetase — (ii) Charges tRNA with the correct amino acid. This enzyme catalyzes the attachment of the appropriate amino acid to its cognate tRNA in an ATP-dependent reaction. (Q) Release factors — (iv) Recognizes stop codons and terminates translation. When a stop codon (UAA, UAG, or UGA) enters the A-site, release factors bind and trigger hydrolysis of the polypeptide from the tRNA. (R) Peptidyl transferase — (i) Catalyzes peptide bond formation. This is a ribozyme activity of the large ribosomal subunit (23S rRNA). (S) EF-Tu — (iii) Delivers aminoacyl-tRNA to the ribosome A-site. EF-Tu is an elongation factor in prokaryotes that brings charged tRNAs to the A-site in a GTP-dependent manner.
subject: biology

elimination_hints.hint: This incorrectly assigns release factors (Q) to delivering aminoacyl-tRNA to the A-site. Release factors recognize stop codons and terminate translation, not deliver tRNAs.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the role of release factors with elongation factors

elimination_hints.hint: This incorrectly assigns aminoacyl-tRNA synthetase (P) to delivering tRNA to the A-site and peptidyl transferase (R) to charging tRNA. Their roles are reversed.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the functions of aminoacyl-tRNA synthetase and EF-Tu

elimination_hints.hint: This completely misassigns functions. Aminoacyl-tRNA synthetase (P) charges tRNA, not forms peptide bonds, and release factors (Q) terminate translation, not charge tRNAs.
elimination_hints.misconception: Fundamental confusion about the roles of different translation components

question_text:
Match the following codons/features with their properties:

Column I:
(P) UUU
(Q) AUG
(R) UAA, UAG, UGA
(S) Tryptophan and Methionine

Column II:
(i) Coded by only one codon each (non-degenerate)
(ii) First codon to be deciphered (codes for phenylalanine)
(iii) Nonsense codons / termination codons
(iv) Dual function — initiation and amino acid coding

Select the correct match:
explanation: The correct matching is: (P) UUU — (ii) First codon to be deciphered. In Nirenberg and Matthaei's landmark experiment (1961), poly-U mRNA in a cell-free system produced polyphenylalanine, establishing that UUU codes for phenylalanine. (Q) AUG — (iv) Dual function. AUG serves as both the initiation codon (start signal) and codes for methionine wherever it appears in the coding sequence. (R) UAA, UAG, UGA — (iii) Nonsense codons. These three codons do not code for any amino acid and instead signal the termination of translation. They are called stop codons or nonsense codons. (S) Tryptophan and Methionine — (i) Non-degenerate. These are the only two amino acids coded by a single codon each (UGG for tryptophan, AUG for methionine), making them the exceptions to degeneracy.
subject: biology

elimination_hints.hint: This incorrectly assigns UUU (P) to non-degenerate codons. UUU codes for phenylalanine and was the first codon deciphered. Phenylalanine has two codons (UUU and UUC), so it is degenerate.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the first decoded codon with non-degenerate amino acids

elimination_hints.hint: This assigns UUU (P) to nonsense codons, which is incorrect. UUU is a sense codon for phenylalanine. Also, AUG (Q) is not the first codon deciphered.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing sense codons with nonsense codons

elimination_hints.hint: This incorrectly assigns AUG (Q) to nonsense codons. AUG is the start codon coding for methionine, not a stop codon. Also, stop codons (R) are not described as having dual function.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing AUG (start codon) with stop codons

question_text: True or False: The genetic code is nearly universal, meaning the same codons code for the same amino acids in almost all organisms from bacteria to humans.
explanation: This statement is TRUE. The genetic code is nearly universal, which means that the same codons code for the same amino acids in almost all organisms — from bacteria and archaea to plants and animals. For example, UUU codes for phenylalanine and AUG codes for methionine in virtually all known organisms. This universality of the genetic code is strong evidence that all life on Earth shares a common evolutionary origin. There are a few minor exceptions, such as some mitochondrial codons and certain protozoans (like Mycoplasma), where slight deviations exist, but the code is overwhelmingly conserved.
subject: biology

elimination_hints.hint: The statement is true. The universality of the genetic code is a well-established property described in NCERT. The same codons specify the same amino acids in nearly all organisms.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking that different organisms use completely different genetic codes

question_text: True or False: The genetic code is overlapping, meaning a single nucleotide can be part of more than one codon during translation of an mRNA.
explanation: This statement is FALSE. The genetic code is non-overlapping, meaning each nucleotide in the mRNA belongs to only one codon and is read only once during translation. In a non-overlapping code, the codons are read sequentially without sharing any nucleotides. For example, in the sequence AUGCCCGAU, the codons are AUG-CCC-GAU, where each nucleotide is part of exactly one codon. If the code were overlapping, a nucleotide could be shared between adjacent codons, but this is not the case. Francis Crick and Sydney Brenner's genetic experiments with T4 bacteriophage confirmed the non-overlapping and commaless nature of the genetic code.
subject: biology

elimination_hints.hint: The statement is false. The genetic code is non-overlapping, not overlapping. Each nucleotide belongs to only one codon and is read only once during translation.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking the genetic code is overlapping when it is actually non-overlapping

question_text: When multiple ribosomes are attached to a single mRNA and translate it simultaneously, the structure formed is called a ______.
explanation: When multiple ribosomes simultaneously translate a single mRNA molecule, the resulting structure is called a polyribosome (also known as a polysome). In a polysome, several ribosomes are spaced along the mRNA, each at a different stage of translation, synthesizing multiple copies of the same polypeptide simultaneously. This greatly increases the efficiency of protein synthesis. The ribosomes closest to the 5' end of mRNA have shorter nascent polypeptide chains, while those near the 3' end have nearly complete polypeptides. Polysomes can be observed using electron microscopy.
subject: biology

elimination_hints.hint: A replicon is the unit of DNA replication that is replicated from a single origin. It is not related to multiple ribosomes on mRNA.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing DNA replication terminology with translation terminology

elimination_hints.hint: A nucleosome is the basic unit of chromatin structure, consisting of DNA wrapped around histone proteins. It has nothing to do with translation.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing chromatin packaging structures with translation structures

elimination_hints.hint: A spliceosome is a complex of snRNPs involved in removing introns from pre-mRNA during RNA processing, not a structure of multiple ribosomes.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing RNA processing machinery with translation machinery

question_text:
A researcher is studying a bacterial mRNA with the following coding sequence: 5'-AUGAAACCCUAG-3'. During an experiment, a point mutation changes the seventh nucleotide (C) to an A. What will be the effect on the translated polypeptide?

Original: 5'-AUG AAA CCC UAG-3'
Mutated: 5'-AUG AAA CAC UAG-3'
explanation: The original mRNA sequence 5'-AUGAAACCCUAG-3' is read as codons: AUG (Met) - AAA (Lys) - CCC (Pro) - UAG (Stop). This produces a tripeptide Met-Lys-Pro. When the seventh nucleotide changes from C to A, the mutated sequence becomes 5'-AUGAAACACUAG-3', read as: AUG (Met) - AAA (Lys) - CAC (His) - UAG (Stop). The third codon changes from CCC (proline) to CAC (histidine). This is a missense mutation — a single nucleotide change that causes one amino acid to be replaced by another. The polypeptide length remains the same (3 amino acids), but proline is replaced by histidine at the third position.
subject: biology

options.text: The polypeptide will be truncated due to a premature stop codon
No change in the polypeptide (silent mutation)
Proline will be replaced by histidine (missense mutation)
The reading frame will be shifted, altering all downstream amino acids

elimination_hints.hint: A premature stop codon would only occur if the mutation created UAA, UAG, or UGA in the reading frame. The mutated codon CAC codes for histidine, not a stop signal.
elimination_hints.misconception: Assuming any mutation will create a stop codon

elimination_hints.hint: A silent mutation occurs when the nucleotide change does not alter the amino acid (due to degeneracy). Here, CCC (Pro) changes to CAC (His) — a different amino acid, so it is not silent.
elimination_hints.misconception: Assuming all point mutations in the third position of a codon are silent

elimination_hints.hint: A frameshift mutation results from insertion or deletion of nucleotides (not a multiple of three). A point mutation (substitution) does not alter the reading frame.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing point mutations (substitutions) with frameshift mutations (insertions/deletions)
